other_observers: Maggie Boswell, Alec Hopping, Kathy Mihm-Dunning and others.
specific_location: Calypso Cascades in Wild Basin, Rocky Mtn Nat Park
description: The bird was a very small stub-tailed Wren which always held its tail nearly upright.  It was somewhat larger than a House Wren and its song was clearly not a House Wren.  I first saw it on the morning of July 4th by following its song, but I only got a very poor and brief look among dark roots of trees.  I returned on July 10th with Maggie and Alec, and the bird was singing when we arrived.  We saw it very briefly on the trail and then followed it first upstream and then downstream.  I obtained very good looks for about 30 seconds twice when it was not too low in trees, and poorer looks at other times.  The bird was almost uniformly a dark brown with the most obvious marks being a lighter (but absolutely not white) eyeline, and very small whitish spots on the upper wing coverts and along the flanks.  The upper breast and throat were slightly lighter than the rest of the bird, but nowhere near white in color: this was seen well the two times I saw it in good light for about 30 seconds.  The bill was dark and the legs were brown.  The attached photo by Alec Hopping is a very good image of what I saw on the bird.  I heard the bird singing on both visits, and the song sounded less rich and sweet than I remember for Winter Wren and European Wren.  Sound recordings were obtained by both Kathy Minh-Dunning and Alec Hopping.
